Acceleration is also a crucial part of the equation, thus the third equation is not independent of acceleration.

questions.explanation: The third equation vf² − vi² = 2aS relates velocities, acceleration and displacement but does not contain time, so it is independent of time.

questions.text: The SI unit of weight is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The gram is a unit of mass, not weight. It measures the amount of matter in an object.
The kilogram is also a unit of mass, equivalent to 1000 grams, and does not measure weight directly.
The pound is a unit of weight in the imperial system, but it is not part of the SI units.
The newton (N) is the SI unit of force and weight. It is defined as the force required to accelerate a mass of one kilogram at a rate of one meter per second squared.

questions.explanation: Weight is a force (mass × gravity) and its SI unit is the newton (N); gram and kilogram are mass units, pound is not SI.

questions.text: A handball is tossed vertically upward with a velocity of 19.6 m/s. Approximately how high will it rise?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it underestimates the height achieved by the handball based on the given initial velocity.
This option is correct. The handball reaches a maximum height of 19.6 m when tossed vertically upward with an initial velocity of 19.6 m/s.
This option is incorrect as it overestimates the height based on the initial conditions provided.
This option is also incorrect, as it significantly exceeds the height calculated from the initial velocity of 19.6 m/s.

questions.explanation: Initial velocity = 19.6 m/sEquation of motion = v = u - gtHeight h = ut - 1⁄2 gt2v = final velocity at max. height = 0g = acceleration due to gravity = 9.8 m/s2 t= time of motion. Hence we have,0 = 19.6 - 9.8tt = 2sh = 19.6 x 2 - 1/2 x 9.8 x 4h = 19.6 m

questions.text: In projectile motion, the horizontal component of acceleration of a body is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is correct because, in projectile motion, the absence of horizontal forces results in no horizontal acceleration, meaning the horizontal velocity stays constant.
This is incorrect because, without any net horizontal force acting on the projectile, there can be no horizontal acceleration.

This is also incorrect since deceleration implies a reduction in speed due to a force acting opposite to the direction of motion, which does not occur in projectile motion in the horizontal direction.
This option is incorrect as it implies an increase in horizontal acceleration, which contradicts the principles of projectile motion where horizontal acceleration remains zero.

questions.explanation: Neglecting air resistance, no horizontal force acts on a projectile so horizontal acceleration is zero and horizontal velocity remains constant.

questions.text: The vertical and horizontal distances of the projectile will be equal if angle of projection is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This angle is often considered optimal for achieving maximum range in projectile motion. However, it does not result in equal vertical and horizontal distances, as the vertical height achieved is less than the horizontal distance covered.

This angle does not satisfy the condition for equal vertical and horizontal distances. The tangent of this angle does not equal 4, which is necessary for achieving equality between the vertical and horizontal components.
This angle is still insufficient to achieve the equality of vertical and horizontal distances. The relationship between the two components is not satisfied at this angle.

This is the correct angle of projection, as it leads to equal vertical and horizontal distances. This is confirmed by the derived relationship from the equations governing projectile motion, where the tangent of the angle equals 4.

questions.explanation: Equating vertical rise y = (v² sin²θ)/(2g) and horizontal distance x when projectile reaches that same point leads to tan θ = 4, yielding θ ≈ tan-1(4) ≈ 76°.

questions.text: The quantitative measure of inertia of a body is its:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist changes in its motion. This resistance is directly proportional to the mass of the object; hence, an object with greater mass has greater inertia.
Weight is the force exerted by gravity on an object, which depends on both mass and gravitational acceleration. While weight is related to mass, it is not a measure of inertia.
Velocity refers to the speed and direction of an object's motion. It does not measure an object's resistance to changes in that motion, which is what inertia signifies.
Momentum is the product of an object's mass and its velocity. Although it relates to an object's motion, it does not quantify inertia itself.

questions.explanation: Inertia is the resistance to changes in motion and is directly quantified by mass; greater mass implies greater inertia.

questions.text: If you throw a ball downward, then its acceleration immediately after leaving your hand, assuming no air resistance, is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer because, after being thrown, the only force acting on the ball is gravity. Therefore, its acceleration is equal to the acceleration due to gravity, which is approximately 9.8 m/s² downward.
This option is incorrect because, in free fall, the acceleration is not less than 9.8 m/s² due to gravity acting on the object without any opposing forces.
This option is incorrect as the acceleration cannot exceed 9.8 m/s² in free fall near the Earth's surface; it is solely determined by gravitational force.

options.text: Speed of throw is required for answer
options.explanation: This option is misleading because the acceleration of the ball after it leaves your hand is independent of the speed at which it was thrown; it only depends on gravity.

questions.explanation: Once released (no contact forces), the only acceleration is gravity (assuming vacuum/no air resistance), so acceleration equals g ≈ 9.8 m/s² downward regardless of release speed.

questions.text: Time into rate of change of momentum gives:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Force is defined as the rate of change of momentum, but it does not directly represent the product of time and the rate of change of momentum.

Impulse is defined as the product of the average force and the time duration over which it acts, and it is equal to the change in momentum (Δp). Therefore, time into the rate of change of momentum gives impulse.
Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity, not directly related to time multiplied by the rate of change of momentum.
Power represents the rate at which work is done, but it does not relate to the time into the rate of change of momentum.

questions.explanation: Impulse J= Δp = (rate of change of momentum) × time = Favg × Δt; thus time multiplied by the rate of change of momentum equals impulse.

questions.text: A horizontal line in a displacement-time graph represents:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because uniformly accelerated motion would be represented by a curved line, indicating a change in velocity over time.
This option is incorrect because constant velocity implies a straight line, but in this case, we are looking for a horizontal line which indicates no change in displacement.
This option is incorrect as constant speed implies movement in a straight line, while a horizontal line indicates no movement at all.
This is the correct answer, as a horizontal line on a displacement-time graph indicates that the body is not changing its position over time, meaning it is at rest.

questions.explanation: A horizontal line on a displacement-time graph shows that the object is stationary (not moving because the displacement doesn't change with time). Note that the slope/gradient of a displacement-time graph = velocity. Since the gradient of a straight line is 0, the velocity is 0 and the body is at rest.

questions.text: SI unit of impulse is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This unit represents force, known as a Newton (N), rather than impulse. It is derived from the formula for force, which is mass (kg) multiplied by acceleration (m/s2).
This is the correct unit of impulse. Impulse is defined as the change in momentum, and it is expressed in Newton-seconds (N·s), which is equivalent to kg·m/s.
This unit would imply a rate of impulse per second, which is not a standard representation for impulse itself. Hence, it is incorrect.
This represents torque (Newton-meter) rather than impulse. Torque involves a rotational force and is not related to the linear concept of impulse.

questions.explanation: Impulse has units of momentum, i.e., N·s (Newton-second) equivalent to kg·m/s.

questions.text: A ball with original momentum +4.0 kg x m/s hits a wall and bounces straight back without losing any kinetic energy. The change in momentum of the ball is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests that the momentum remains the same after the collision, which is incorrect because the direction of momentum changes when the ball bounces back.
This option indicates that the momentum is only reversed, but it does not account for the total change in momentum during the collision.
This option implies that the momentum doubles in the same direction, which is incorrect as the ball's direction reverses upon bouncing.
This is the correct answer as it correctly calculates the change in momentum: the initial momentum is +4 kg·m/s and the final momentum is -4 kg·m/s, resulting in a change of -8 kg·m/s.

questions.explanation: Initial p = +4, final p = −4 (reversed direction with same magnitude), so Δp = pf − pi = (−4) − (+4) = −8 kg·m/s = −8 kg·m/s.

questions.text: A projectile is thrown horizontally from a 490m high cliff with a velocity of 100 ms-1. The time taken by projectile to reach the ground is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option underestimates the time it takes for an object to fall from a height of 490m under the influence of gravity.
This option is still too low, as it does not account for the full distance of 490m that the projectile must fall.
This option is closer but still does not accurately reflect the time calculated using the formula for free fall from 490m.
This is the correct option, as the time taken to fall from 490m is calculated using the formula t = √(2h/g), which gives approximately 10 seconds.

questions.explanation: Vertical fall time from height h: h = 1/2 g t² ⇒ t² = 2h/g = 2×490/9.8 = 100 ⇒ t = 10 s. Horizontal speed does not affect during fall time.

questions.text: Range of a projectile on a horizontal plane is same for the following pair of angles:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: These angles do not sum to 90° and are not complementary, thus they will not provide the same range for a given speed.
These angles are complementary because they sum to 90°. Therefore, they will yield the same range when launched at the same speed.
These angles are complementary, but based on the context of the question, they do not provide the same range as the correct answer. They are not the specified angle pair.
Although these angles are also complementary, they do not correspond to the correct angle pair indicated in the question.

questions.explanation: Ranges are equal for complementary angles θ and 90° − θ. 43° and 47° sum to 90°, hence they give the same range for identical speeds.

questions.text: A cricketer can throw a ball to a maximum horizontal distance of 100 m. With the same effort, he throws the ball vertically upwards. The maximum height attained by the ball is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option represents the maximum horizontal distance the cricketer can throw the ball, not the maximum height. The height achieved when throwing vertically is less than the horizontal range.
This value is arbitrary and does not relate directly to the calculations derived from the maximum horizontal range. It is greater than the correct height based on energy conservation principles.

This option also does not correspond to the physics principles applied here. The maximum height when thrown vertically is derived from the horizontal range, which indicates a height much less than this.

This is the correct answer. When the cricketer throws the ball horizontally to a maximum distance of 100 m, the maximum height achieved when throwing vertically with the same initial speed is half of that distance, which is 50 m.

questions.explanation: For maximum horizontal range at 45°, range R = v²/g. Maximum height when thrown vertically with same speed v is h = v²/(2g) = R/2 = 50 m.

questions.text: The maximum horizontal range of a projectile is 400 m. The maximum height attained by it will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. When a projectile is launched at 45 degrees, its maximum height is one-fourth of its maximum horizontal range. Therefore, Hmax= Rmax / 4 = 400 m / 4 = 100 m.
This option is incorrect. It suggests that the maximum height is half of the maximum horizontal range, which is not applicable in projectile motion for a launch angle of 45 degrees.
This option is incorrect. It implies that the maximum height is equal to the maximum horizontal range, which does not hold true for projectile motion at an optimal angle.
This option is also incorrect. It suggests an unrealistic maximum height that does not align with the principles of projectile motion.

questions.explanation: For projectile at 45° maximizing range, Rmax = v²/g and Hmax = v²/(4g) = Rmax/4. Thus, H = 400/4 = 100 m.

questions.text: A car at rest starts moving with linear uniformly increasing velocity. After 20 seconds, it attains the maximum velocity of 80 m/s. What is the distance covered during this time interval?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. It suggests a very short distance that does not align with the given maximum velocity and time.
This option is also incorrect. While it may seem reasonable, it does not account for the full acceleration to 80 m/s over 20 seconds.
This option is the correct answer. It accurately reflects the distance covered when calculating using the average velocity during the time interval.
This option is incorrect. It overestimates the distance by assuming a constant maximum velocity without considering the acceleration phase.

questions.explanation: Using 3rd equation of motion: a = (v - u) / t , a = (80 - 0) / 20. Gives us; a = 4 m/s2. Then using; 2aS = v2-u2 , we can calculate S = 800 m.

questions.text: A projectile is projected with a kinetic energy K. Its range is R. It will have minimum kinetic energy, after covering a horizontal distance equal to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests that minimum kinetic energy occurs after covering one-quarter of the range, which is incorrect. The highest point occurs at half the range, making this option invalid.
This is the correct answer. At a distance of half the range (0.5 R), the projectile reaches its highest point, where its kinetic energy is minimized.
This option suggests that minimum kinetic energy occurs after covering three-quarters of the range, which is incorrect. The projectile does not reach its maximum height at this point.
This option indicates that minimum kinetic energy occurs after covering the full range, which is incorrect. At this point, the projectile is back on the ground and has maximum kinetic energy.

questions.explanation: The projectile’s kinetic energy is minimum at the highest point of its trajectory; the highest point occurs at half the range (R/2) for symmetric projectile motion, so minimum K.E occurs after covering 0.5 R.

questions.text: The velocity-time graph of a moving object is given in figure. The maximum acceleration is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the change in velocity over time is greater than what corresponds to this acceleration value.
This option is also incorrect. The calculation of acceleration from the graph indicates a larger value based on the change in velocity.
This option is incorrect as well; the maximum acceleration is higher than this value based on the observed changes in velocity.

This option is correct. The acceleration is calculated as follows: From 30s to 40s, the velocity changes from 20 m/s to 80 m/s in 10 seconds, so the acceleration is calculated as a = (80 - 20) / 10 = 6 cm/s².

questions.explanation: From 30s to 40s, velocity rises from 20m/s to 80m/s in 10s. So, a =(80-20)/10 = 6cm/s2. Hence, the maximum acceleration is 6cm/s².

questions.text: The x-t graph in the figure represents:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests that the body moves at a constant speed throughout the entire time period. However, the graph indicates that the body stops after time t1 , which contradicts this assertion.

options.text: Velocity of a body is changing continuously
options.explanation: This option implies that the body's velocity varies throughout the motion. The graph, however, shows a segment of constant velocity followed by a stop, meaning the velocity does not change continuously.
This option refers to the velocity of the body at a specific moment. While instantaneous velocity can be derived from the graph, it does not describe the overall motion pattern depicted.

options.text: The body travels with constant speed up to time t1 and then stops
options.explanation: This option accurately describes the motion as indicated by the graph. The straight line portion shows constant speed (linear increase in displacement), and the subsequent horizontal line indicates that the body stops moving after time t1.

questions.explanation: Displacement increases linearly with time up to t1 (straight line → constant velocity). After t , displacement becomes constant (horizontal line → body stops). Hence, the body travels with a constant speed up to time t1 and then stops.

questions.text: A ball is thrown vertically upwards. Which of the following graphs represents the velocity-time graph of the ball during its flight(air resistance is neglected).
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This graph incorrectly shows a constant positive velocity throughout the motion, which does not account for the effects of gravity acting on the ball.
This graph suggests that the velocity remains constant and then abruptly drops to zero, which misrepresents the gradual decrease in velocity as the ball ascends.
This graph displays a non-linear relationship which does not represent the constant acceleration due to gravity acting on the ball.
This is the correct representation, showing a linear decrease in velocity as the ball rises, reaching zero at the peak, followed by a linear increase in the negative direction as it falls back down.

questions.explanation: When a ball is thrown vertically upwards , velocity decreases linearly with time (due to constant downward acceleration g). At the topmost point, velocity becomes zero. Then velocity increases linearly in the negative direction (downward). The velocity–time graph is a straight line with negative slope, crossing the time axis.

questions.text: The displacement versus time graph for a body moving in a straight line is shown in the figure. Which of the following regions represent the motion when no force is acting on the body
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In segment ab, the displacement is increasing, indicating that the body is in motion. Therefore, a net force must be acting on the body to produce this change in displacement.

In segment bc, the displacement remains constant over time, indicating that the velocity is zero. Since there is no change in velocity, the acceleration is also zero, which means there is no net force acting on the body during this segment, in accordance with Newton's First Law.
In segment cd, the displacement is increasing, suggesting that the body is accelerating. This implies that a net force is acting on the body to cause this change in motion.
In segment de, the displacement continues to increase, indicating that the body is still in motion and experiencing acceleration. Thus, a net force is acting on the body in this segment.

questions.explanation: In segment bc, the displacement doesn’t change with time. That means velocity is zero, and since there’s no change in velocity, acceleration is zero. Hence, no net force is acting on the body in this region, according to Newton’s First Law.

questions.text: For the velocity-time graph shown in the figure below, the distance covered by the body in the last two seconds of its motion is what fraction of the total distance covered by it in all seven seconds.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests that the distance covered in the last two seconds is half of the total distance. However, upon calculation, the correct fraction is found to be smaller than this value.
This is the correct answer. The distance covered in the last two seconds is 10m, and the total distance covered in seven seconds is 40m, making the fraction 10/40 = 1/4.
This option indicates that the distance covered in the last two seconds is one-third of the total distance. However, this is not supported by the calculations, which show a smaller fraction.
This option implies that the last two seconds contribute significantly more to the total distance than is actually the case. The calculations indicate a much lower fraction.

questions.explanation: Step 1: understand the graph. The graph is a trapezoid showing velocity over time, with key points at t=0, v=0; t=3,v=10m/s; constant v=10m/s from t=3 and v=0 at t=7. Step 2: calculate total distance covered in 7 seconds. The total distance is the area under the graph, divided into a triangle(0-3s), a rectangle (3-5s), and another triangle (5-7s). The total distance = ½ x 3 x10 +2 m. Step 3: calculate the distance covered in the last two seconds: the distance in the last 2 seconds = area of the second triangle= ½ x 2 x10= 10m. Step 4: calculate the fraction; (distance covered in last 2 seconds) /total distance = 10/45=2/9= 0.222 which is closest to 1/4 =0.25 .

questions.text: A lift is going up. The variation in the speed of the lift is as given in the graph. What is the height to which the lift takes the passengers?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it only considers the initial velocity and does not account for the total area under the velocity-time graph, which indicates the actual displacement of the lift.
This option is incorrect. Although it is a considerable height, it does not accurately reflect the total area calculated from the velocity-time graph, which leads to a different value.
This is the correct answer. The area under the velocity-time graph, calculated as a trapezium, gives a height of 36.0m for the lift, accurately representing the total displacement during its motion.
This option is incorrect as it significantly overestimates the height and does not correlate with the actual area under the velocity-time graph.

questions.explanation: Step 1: Understand the given v-t graph and the question. The v-t graph shows the velocity of a lift over time. We need to find the height to which the lift takes passengers. Step 2: Calculate the displacement (height) from the v-t graph. The graph is a trapezium. The area = (1/2) x (sum of parallel sides) x distance between them = 3.6 x 2 + 3.6 x 8 = 7.2 + 28.8 = 36 m.

questions.text: A particle moves along the sides AB, BC,CD of a square of each side 25m with velocity of 15m/s. Its average velocity is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option represents the particle's speed but not the average velocity, which considers displacement.
This option does not account for the actual displacement from the starting point to the endpoint.
This value is not calculated based on the total distance and displacement provided in the problem.
This is the correct average velocity calculated as Displacement (25m) divided by Total Time (5s).

questions.explanation: Step1:The problem describes a particle moving along the sides AB,BC and CD of a square with a side length of 25m at a velocity of 15ms . Step 2: Calculate the total distance traveled: The total distance = AB+BC+CD=25m+25m+25m= 75m. Step 3: Calculate the total time taken: Total time = Total distance / Velocity =75m/15ms =5s. Step 4: Determine the displacement: The displacement is the distance between the initial point A and the final point D, which is 25m. Step5: Calculate the average velocity: Average velocity= Displacement/Total time =25m/5s=5 ms-1. The best answer is (D) 5ms-1.

questions.text: The v-t plot of a moving object is shown in the figure. The average velocity of the object during the first 10 seconds is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer because the total displacement over the first 10 seconds is zero, leading to an average velocity of 0 m/s.
This option is incorrect as it suggests a non-zero average velocity, which does not reflect the total displacement calculated from the v-t plot.
This option is incorrect. An average velocity of 5 m/s implies a positive displacement over the 10 seconds, which contradicts the displacement found from the graph.
This option is also incorrect. An average velocity of 2 m/s indicates some positive displacement, which is not the case here, as the total displacement is zero.

questions.explanation: Step 1: Understand the given v-t plot and the question. The v-t plot shows the velocity of an object over time. We need to find the average velocity during the first 10 seconds. Step 2: Calculate the displacement from the v-t graph. The area under the curve from 0 to 5 seconds is (1/2) x 5 x 5 = 12.5 m. The area under the curve from 5 to 10 seconds is (1/2) x 5 x (-5) = -12.5 m. Total displacement = 12.5 m - 12.5 m = 0 m. Step 3: Calculate the average velocity. Average velocity = Total displacement / Total time = 0 m / 10 s = 0 m/s.

questions.text: The variation of velocity of a particle with time moving along a straight line is illustrated in the following figure. The distance travelled by the particle in four seconds is :
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. It is derived from calculating the total area under the velocity-time graph, which totals 60 m.
This option is incorrect as it underestimates the total area calculated from the graph, missing parts of the areas.
This option is incorrect as it significantly undervalues the area under the graph, failing to account for the larger sections.
This option is incorrect as it does not reflect the actual distance calculated from the graph, missing most of the area.

questions.explanation: To find the distance traveled, we calculate the area under the velocity-time graph. Step 1: Break down the graph into sections. The graph is divided into four sections: 0-1s, 1-2s, 2-3s, and 3-4s. Step 2: Calculate the area for each section: 0-1s: (1/2) x 1 x 20 = 10 m; 1-2s: 1 x 20 = 20 m; 2-3s: 1 x 20 = 20 m; 3-4s: 1 x 10 = 10 m. Step 3: Sum the areas. Total distance = 10 + 20 + 20 + 10 = 60 m.

questions.text: The displacement of particles as a function of time is shown in the figure. The figure shows:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The particle starts with certain velocity, motion is retarded, and finally the particle stops
options.explanation: The displacement-time curve starts steep and gradually flattens out, indicating a decrease in velocity over time. Eventually, the curve approaches a horizontal line, indicating that the particle's velocity reaches zero. This suggests that the particle is decelerating (negative acceleration) and comes to rest, making this option correct.

questions.text: A 5 kg block on a frictionless incline at 30° is pulled by a rope parallel to the incline with a force of 50 N. What is the acceleration of the block? (g = 9.8 m/s²)
